subject: Physics
q: A simple pendulum with length 100 cm and bob of mass 250 g is executing S.H.M of amplitude 10 cm. The maximum tension in the string is found to be x40 N. The value of x is _____.
question: A simple pendulum with length 100 cm and bob of mass 250 g is executing S.H.M of amplitude 10 cm. The maximum tension in the string is found to be x40 N. The value of x is _____.
solution:
From the given figure we can see that 

sinθ0=Al=10100=110

From conservation of energy mv22=mgl(1-cosθ0)

Maximum tension occurs at mean position

T=mg+mv2l⇒T=mg+2mg(1-cosθ0)

Substituting the values,

T=mg(1+2(1-1-sin2θ0)⇒T=mg(3-21-1100)⇒T=0.25×9.8(3-2(1-1200))⇒T=0.25×9.8×1.01=2.4745 N

It is given that

 T=x40⇒x=40×2.4745=98.98≈99

subject: Physics
q: Two bodies of masses 1 kg and 4 kg are connected to a vertical spring, as shown in the figure. The smaller mass executes simple harmonic motion of angular frequency 25 rad s-1, and amplitude 1.6 cm while the bigger mass remains stationary on the ground. The maximum force exerted by the system on the floor is (take g = 10 m s-2).
question: Two bodies of masses 1 kg and 4 kg are connected to a vertical spring, as shown in the figure. The smaller mass executes simple harmonic motion of angular frequency 25 rad s-1, and amplitude 1.6 cm while the bigger mass remains stationary on the ground. The maximum force exerted by the system on the floor is (take g = 10 m s-2).
options: 20 N
60 N
40 N
10 N
solution:
Given ω = 2 5 rad/s and A = 1.6 cm

∴   ω=km=25 ⇒k= 25 2

Maximum compression in the spring

=xo+A

=mgk+A

∴  Fmax=4g + k(x0 + A)

Fmax =4g+kmgk+A

            = 40 + 20 = 60 N

subject: Physics
q: The displacement of a particle executing SHM is given by $x=10 \sin \left(w t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) m$. The time period of motion is $3.14 \mathrm{~s}$. The velocity of the particle at $t=0$ is ______$\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: The displacement of a particle executing SHM is given by $x=10 \sin \left(w t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) m$. The time period of motion is $3.14 \mathrm{~s}$. The velocity of the particle at $t=0$ is ______$\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
Given,
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T}=3.14=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega} \\
& \omega=2 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \mathrm{x}=10 \sin \left(\omega \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \\
& \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=10 \omega \cos \left(\omega \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \\
& \text { at } \mathrm{t}=0 \\
& \mathrm{v}=10 \omega \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=10 \times 2 \times \frac{1}{2}[\mathrm{using} \omega=2 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}] \\
& \mathrm{v}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two simple pendulums having lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$ with negligible string mass undergo angular displacements $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, from their mean positions, respectively. If the angular accelerations of both pendulums are same, then which expression is correct?
question: Two simple pendulums having lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$ with negligible string mass undergo angular displacements $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, from their mean positions, respectively. If the angular accelerations of both pendulums are same, then which expression is correct?
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \omega=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{g}}{\ell}} \\ & \alpha=-\omega^2 \theta \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{~g}}{\ell_1} \theta_1=\frac{\mathrm{g}}{\ell_2} \theta_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \theta_1 \ell_2=\theta_2 \ell_2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A point mass oscillates along the $x$-axis according to the law $\mathrm{x=x_0 \cos (\omega t-\pi / 4)}$. If the acceleration of the particle is written as
$\mathrm{a=A \cos (\omega t+\delta)}$
question:
A point mass oscillates along the $x$-axis according to the law $\mathrm{x=x_0 \cos (\omega t-\pi / 4)}$. If the acceleration of the particle is written as
$\mathrm{a=A \cos (\omega t+\delta)}$
solution:
$\mathrm{v=-x_0 \omega \sin (\omega t-\pi / 4)}$
$\mathrm{a=-x_0 \omega^2 \cos \left(\omega t+\pi-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}$
$\mathrm{a=A \cos (\omega t+\delta)}$
$\mathrm{A=x_0 \omega^2 ; \quad \delta=\frac{3 \pi}{4}}$

subject: Physics
q: A particle executes S.H.M. of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, the position of the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> and it moves along positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. The displacement of particle in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mi>δ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>δ</mi></math> will be
question: A particle executes S.H.M. of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, the position of the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> and it moves along positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. The displacement of particle in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mi>δ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>δ</mi></math> will be
solution:
Given, the instantaneous equation of motion of the particle is

x=A sinωt+δ   ...1

As the particle is moving along positive x-axis, v=dxdt>0.

Substitute 0 for t and A2 for x into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required phase difference.

A2=Asinω×0+δ⇒sinδ=12=sinπ6⇒δ=π6

subject: Physics
q:
In a linear Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

(A) Restoring force is directly proportional to the displacement.

(B) The acceleration and displacement are opposite in direction.

(C) The velocity is maximum at mean position.

(D) The acceleration is minimum at extreme points.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
In a linear Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

(A) Restoring force is directly proportional to the displacement.

(B) The acceleration and displacement are opposite in direction.

(C) The velocity is maximum at mean position.

(D) The acceleration is minimum at extreme points.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The equations of SHM is given by

F=–kx

⇒a=–ω2x

Velocity is given by, v=ωA2-x2.

Clearly, velocity is maximum at mean position and acceleration is maximum at extreme positions.

Hence, this is the correct option.

q: A mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is suspended from a spring of negligible mass and the system oscillates with a frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The frequency of oscillations if a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mi>m</mi></math> is suspended from the same spring is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is _____ .
question: A mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is suspended from a spring of negligible mass and the system oscillates with a frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The frequency of oscillations if a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mi>m</mi></math> is suspended from the same spring is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is _____ .
solution:
Frequency of oscillation of a spring-block system is given by, f=12πkm

Therefore, for both cases, we can write

f1=12πkm and 

f2=12πk9m

Required ratio, f1f2=91=31

subject: Physics
q:
An ideal gas enclosed in a vertical cylindrical container supports a freely moving piston of mass M. The piston and the cylinder have equal cross-sectional area A. When the piston is in equilibrium, the volume of the gas is V0 and its pressure is M0. The piston is slightly displaced from the equilibrium position and released. Assuming that the system is completely isolated from its surrounding, the piston executes a simple harmonic motion with frequency
[Assume the system is in space.]
question:
An ideal gas enclosed in a vertical cylindrical container supports a freely moving piston of mass M. The piston and the cylinder have equal cross-sectional area A. When the piston is in equilibrium, the volume of the gas is V0 and its pressure is M0. The piston is slightly displaced from the equilibrium position and released. Assuming that the system is completely isolated from its surrounding, the piston executes a simple harmonic motion with frequency
[Assume the system is in space.]
solution:
For an adiabatic process,

Pvγ=constant

Differentiating with respect to v,

dPdvvγ+Pγvγ-1=0

dP=-γPvdv=-γPvAdx



Thus, the acceleration, a=dFM=γPA2MVdx

Comparing with ,  a=-ω2dx, we get,

ω=γP0A2MV0=2πf


f=12πγP0A2MV0

subject: Physics
q: Choose the correct length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>L</mi><mo>)</mo></math> versus square of time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>)</mo></math> ) graph for a simple pendulum executing simple harmonic motion.
question: Choose the correct length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>L</mi><mo>)</mo></math> versus square of time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>)</mo></math> ) graph for a simple pendulum executing simple harmonic motion.
solution:
A simple pendulum consists of a point mass attached to an inextensible string and suspended from a support.

The formula to calculate the time period T of a simple pendulum is given by

T=2πlg.......................(1)

where, l is the length of the pendulum and g is the acceleration due to gravity.

Simplify equation (1) to obtain the relation between the time period and the length.

T2=4π2g×𝓁T2∝l............................(2)

Equation (2) suggests that the graph between the square of the time period versus the length of the pendulum is a straight line passing through the origin.

q: For a simple harmonic motion in a mass spring system shown, the surface is frictionless. When the mass of the block is 1 kg, the angular frequency is ω1. When the mass block is 2 kg the angular frequency is ω2. The ratio ω2ω1 is :
question: For a simple harmonic motion in a mass spring system shown, the surface is frictionless. When the mass of the block is 1 kg, the angular frequency is ω1. When the mass block is 2 kg the angular frequency is ω2. The ratio ω2ω1 is :
solution:
In case of SHM in spring-block system, angular frequency is given by, ω=km.

Therefore,

ω2ω1=m1m2=12

q: The equation of a particle executing simple harmonic motion is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>π</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, the speed of particle will be (Given: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></math>)
question: The equation of a particle executing simple harmonic motion is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>π</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, the speed of particle will be (Given: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></math>)
solution:
Given, x=sinπt+π3

Velocity  v=dxdt=πcosπt+π3

Now, at t=1 s, v=πcosπ+π3

 v=-π2=-3.142=-1.57 m s-1⇒v=157 cm s-1

subject: Physics
q: In figure (A), mass 2m is fixed on mass m which is attached to two springs of spring constant k. In figure (B), mass m is attached to two spring of spring constant k and 2k. If mass m in (A) and (B) are displaced by distance x horizontally and then released, then time period T1 and T2 corresponding to (A) and (B) respectively follow the relation.
question: In figure (A), mass 2m is fixed on mass m which is attached to two springs of spring constant k. In figure (B), mass m is attached to two spring of spring constant k and 2k. If mass m in (A) and (B) are displaced by distance x horizontally and then released, then time period T1 and T2 corresponding to (A) and (B) respectively follow the relation.
solution:
The time period of spring block system is given by T=2πmk.

In both the cases springs are connected in parallel,

In case 1: keq=2k and total mass is 3m. Therefore,

 T1=2π3mkeq=2π3m2k

In case 2: keq=3k and total mass is m. Therefore,

T2=2πm3k

⇒T1T2=92=32

subject: Physics
q: In the given figure, a mass M is attached to a horizontal spring which is fixed on one side to a rigid support. The spring constant of the spring is k. The mass oscillates on a frictionless surface with time period T and amplitude A. When the mass is in equilibrium position, as shown in the figure, another mass m is gently fixed upon it. The new amplitude of oscillation will be:
question: In the given figure, a mass M is attached to a horizontal spring which is fixed on one side to a rigid support. The spring constant of the spring is k. The mass oscillates on a frictionless surface with time period T and amplitude A. When the mass is in equilibrium position, as shown in the figure, another mass m is gently fixed upon it. The new amplitude of oscillation will be:
solution:
Momentum of system remains conserved.

pi=pf

MAω=m+MA'ω

MAkM=m+MA'km+M

A'=AMM+m

subject: Physics
q: A particle executes simple harmonic motion with an amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. At the mean position, velocity of the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The distance of the particle from the mean position when its speed becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>α</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion with an amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. At the mean position, velocity of the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The distance of the particle from the mean position when its speed becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>α</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
solution:
The formula to calculate the velocity of the particle at the mean position is given by

V=Aω   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

10=4×ω⇒ω=52 rad s-1

The formula to calculate the velocity of the particle at any instantaneous position x can be expressed as

v=ωA2-x2   ...2

From equation (2), it follows that

5=5242-x2⇒x2=16-4=12⇒x=12 cm

Hence, α=12.

subject: Physics
q: Bob of a simple pendulum of length $l$ is made of iron. The pendulum is oscillating over a horizontal coil carrying direct current. If the time period of the pendulum is $\mathrm{T}$ then :
question: Bob of a simple pendulum of length $l$ is made of iron. The pendulum is oscillating over a horizontal coil carrying direct current. If the time period of the pendulum is $\mathrm{T}$ then :
options: $T < 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$ and damping is smaller than in air alone.
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$ and damping is larger than in air alone.
$T>2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$ and damping is smaller than in air alone.
$T < 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$ and damping is larger than in air alone.
solution:
When the pendulum is oscillating over a current carrying coil, and when the direction of oscillating pendulum bob is opposite to the direction of current. Its instantaneous acceleration increases.
Hence time period $\mathrm{T} < 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\ell}{\mathrm{g}}}$ and damping is larger than in air alone due energy dissipation.

subject: Physics
q: Two simple pendulums of length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ respectively are both given small displacement in the same direction at the same instant. They will be again in phase after the shorter pendulum has completed number of oscillations equal to:
question: Two simple pendulums of length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ respectively are both given small displacement in the same direction at the same instant. They will be again in phase after the shorter pendulum has completed number of oscillations equal to:
solution:
Let $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the time period of the two pendulums $\mathrm{T}_1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{\mathrm{~g}}}$ and $\mathrm{T}_2=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{4}{\mathrm{~g}}}$ As $\ell_1 < \ell_2$ therefore $\mathrm{T}_1 < \mathrm{T}_2$ Let at $\mathrm{t}=0$ they start swinging together. Since their time periods are different, the swinging will not be in unison always. Only when number of completed oscillations
differ by an integer, the two pendulums will again begin to swing together Let longer length pendulum complete $\mathrm{n}$ oscillation and shorter length pendulum complete $(n+1)$ oscillation. For unison swinging
$$
\begin{aligned}
& (n+1) T_1=n_2 \\
& (n+1) \times 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{g}}=(n) \times 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{4}{g}} \\
& \Rightarrow n=1 \\
& \therefore n+1=1+1=2
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A particle oscillates along the $x$-axis according to the law, $x(\mathrm{t})=x_0 \sin ^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{t}}{2}\right)$ where $x_0=1 \mathrm{~m}$. The kinetic energy $(\mathrm{K})$ of the particle as a function of $x$ is correctly represented by the graph
question: A particle oscillates along the $x$-axis according to the law, $x(\mathrm{t})=x_0 \sin ^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{t}}{2}\right)$ where $x_0=1 \mathrm{~m}$. The kinetic energy $(\mathrm{K})$ of the particle as a function of $x$ is correctly represented by the graph
solution:
$x(t)=x_0 \sin ^2\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)=\frac{x_0}{2}(1-\cos t)$
Clearly $\frac{x_0}{2}$ is mean position.

subject: Physics
q: A pendulum is executing simple harmonic motion and its maximum kinetic energy is $\mathrm{K}_{1}$. If the length of the pendulum is doubled and it performs simple harmonic motion with the same amplitude as in the first case, its maximum kinetic energy is $\mathrm{K}_{2}$
question: A pendulum is executing simple harmonic motion and its maximum kinetic energy is $\mathrm{K}_{1}$. If the length of the pendulum is doubled and it performs simple harmonic motion with the same amplitude as in the first case, its maximum kinetic energy is $\mathrm{K}_{2}$
solution:
$K=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} x^{2}$

$\Rightarrow K_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} A^{2}$

A $=L \theta$

$\omega=\sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$

$\Rightarrow K=\frac{1}{2} m \cdot \frac{g}{L} \cdot L^{2} \theta^{2}$

$=\frac{1}{2} m g L \theta^{2}$

$\therefore \frac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\frac{L}{2 L}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow K_{2}=2 K_{1}$

$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{K}_{1}}{\mathrm{~K}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2 \mathrm{~L}}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{2}=2 \mathrm{~K}_{1}$

subject: Physics
q: The amplitude of a mass-spring system, which is executing simple harmonic motion decreases with time. If mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>, Decay constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> then how much time is required for the amplitude of the system to drop to half of its initial value? <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>ln</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>693</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: The amplitude of a mass-spring system, which is executing simple harmonic motion decreases with time. If mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>, Decay constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> then how much time is required for the amplitude of the system to drop to half of its initial value? <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>ln</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>693</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
A=A0e-γt=A0e-bt2m

A02=A0e-bt2m

bt2m=ln2

t=2mbln2=2×500×0.69320

t=34.65 second.

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is connected to a spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math>. It is oscillating along <math><mi>x</mi></math>-direction about its equilibrium position in the horizontal plane, taken to be at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, with an amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. An electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is applied along the <math><mi>x</mi></math>-direction. Which of the following statements is correct?
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is connected to a spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math>. It is oscillating along <math><mi>x</mi></math>-direction about its equilibrium position in the horizontal plane, taken to be at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, with an amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. An electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is applied along the <math><mi>x</mi></math>-direction. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The total energy of the system is  <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>q</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>

The new equilibrium position is at a distance  <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>q</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>  from  <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mn>0</mn></math>

The new equilibrium position is at a distance  <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>q</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>k</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>  from  <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mn>0</mn></math>

The total energy of the system is  <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>q</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> .
solution: The equilibrium position will shift to a point where the resultant force is equal to zero. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><msub><mi>x</mi><mrow><mi>e</mi><mi>q</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>q</mi><mi>E</mi><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mo>⇒</mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mrow><mi>e</mi><mi>q</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>q</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow><mi>k</mi></mfrac></math>. Energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><msup><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mrow><mi>q</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow><mi>k</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><mi>k</mi></mfrac></math>.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: A second's pendulum has a time period of 1 second.

Statement II: It takes precisely one second to move between the two extreme positions. In the light of the above statements,

choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: A second's pendulum has a time period of 1 second.

Statement II: It takes precisely one second to move between the two extreme positions. In the light of the above statements,

choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are False
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false
solution: Second pendulum has a time period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> so statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> is false but from one extreme to other it takes only half the time period so statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> is true.

q: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion with time period 2 s and amplitude 1 cm . If D and d are the total distance and displacement covered by the particle in 12.5 s , then $\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}$ is
question: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion with time period 2 s and amplitude 1 cm . If D and d are the total distance and displacement covered by the particle in 12.5 s , then $\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}$ is
solution:
A = 1 cm
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}=\frac{12.5}{2}=6.25 \text { cycles } \\ & \therefore \mathrm{D}=4 \times 6+1=25 \\ & \mathrm{~d}=1 \\ & \frac{D}{d}=25\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: At a given point of time the value of displacement of a simple harmonic oscillator is given as y=A cos30°. If amplitude is 40 cm and kinetic energy at that time is 200 J, the value of force constant 1.0×10x N m–1. The value of x is _____.
question: At a given point of time the value of displacement of a simple harmonic oscillator is given as y=A cos30°. If amplitude is 40 cm and kinetic energy at that time is 200 J, the value of force constant 1.0×10x N m–1. The value of x is _____.
solution:
The formula to calculate the kinetic energy K of a particle executing SHM is given by

K=12kA2-y2   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the value of the unknown parameter.

K=12×k×A2-A2cos230°= 12×k×A2sin230°⇒200=12×1.0×10x×0.42×122= 2×10x100⇒102=10x-2⇒x-2=2⇒x=4

subject: Physics
q:
A uniform disc of radius $R$ and mass $M$ is free to oscillate about the axis $A$ as shown in the figure. For small oscillations the time period is ______.
($g$ is acceleration due to gravity)
question:
A uniform disc of radius $R$ and mass $M$ is free to oscillate about the axis $A$ as shown in the figure. For small oscillations the time period is ______.
($g$ is acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
From the given figure, the axis of rotation is a horizontal line passing through the point $A$ on the edge of the disc. The circular arrow around the axis indicates that the disc oscillates out of its plane, meaning the axis of rotation is a tangent to the disc in its own plane.

The moment of inertia of a uniform disc about its diameter is $I_{cm} = \dfrac{1}{4}MR^2$.

Using the parallel axis theorem, the moment of inertia of the disc about the given axis of rotation is:
$I = I_{cm} + MR^2 = \dfrac{1}{4}MR^2 + MR^2 = \dfrac{5}{4}MR^2$

The distance from the center of mass of the disc to the axis of rotation is $d = R$.

The time period of a physical pendulum is given by:
$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{I}{Mgd}}$

Substituting the values of $I$ and $d$:
$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{\dfrac{5}{4}MR^2}{MgR}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{5R}{4g}}$

Answer: $2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{5R}{4g}}$

subject: Physics
q: A simple pendulum of length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ is oscillating with an angular frequency $10 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. The support of the pendulum starts oscillating up and down with a small angular frequency of $1 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ and an amplitude of $10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$. The relative change in the angular frequency of the pendulum is best given by :
question: A simple pendulum of length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ is oscillating with an angular frequency $10 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. The support of the pendulum starts oscillating up and down with a small angular frequency of $1 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ and an amplitude of $10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$. The relative change in the angular frequency of the pendulum is best given by :
solution:
Angular frequency of pendulum $\omega=\sqrt{\frac{g}{\ell}}$

$\therefore$ relative change in angular frequency

$\frac{\Delta \omega}{\omega}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta \mathrm{g}}{\mathrm{g}}[$ as length remains constant $]$

$\Delta g=2 \mathrm{~A} \omega_{s}^{2}\left[\omega_{s}=\right.$ angular frequency of support and, $\mathrm{A}=$

amplitude]

$\frac{\Delta \omega}{\omega}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 \mathrm{~A} \omega_{\mathrm{S}}^{2}}{\mathrm{~g}}$

$\Delta \omega=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 \times 1^{2} \times 10^{-2}}{10}=10^{-3} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$

q: Two charges, each equal to <math> <mrow> <mtext>q</mtext> </mrow> </math>, are kept at <math> <mrow> <mtext>x</mtext> <mo>=</mo> <mo>-</mo> <mtext>a</mtext> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <mtext>x</mtext> <mo> = </mo> <mtext>a</mtext> </mrow> </math> on the x-axis. A particle of mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math> and charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mtext>q</mtext><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mtext>q</mtext><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>  is placed at the origin. If charge <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>q</mtext> <mn>0</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> is given a small displacement <math> <mrow> <mtext>(y << a)</mtext> </mrow> </math> along the y-axis, the net force acting on the particle is proportional to :
question: Two charges, each equal to <math> <mrow> <mtext>q</mtext> </mrow> </math>, are kept at <math> <mrow> <mtext>x</mtext> <mo>=</mo> <mo>-</mo> <mtext>a</mtext> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <mtext>x</mtext> <mo> = </mo> <mtext>a</mtext> </mrow> </math> on the x-axis. A particle of mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math> and charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mtext>q</mtext><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mtext>q</mtext><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>  is placed at the origin. If charge <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>q</mtext> <mn>0</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> is given a small displacement <math> <mrow> <mtext>(y << a)</mtext> </mrow> </math> along the y-axis, the net force acting on the particle is proportional to :
solution:
⇒ F net = 2Fcos θ

Fnet=2kqq2y2+a22yy2+a2

Fnet=2kqq2yy2+a23/2≈kq2a3y⇒F∝-y

Net force F is opposite the direction of displacement y. Hence, negative sign is used.

subject: Physics
q: Two springs, of force constants $k_1$ and $k_2$, are connected to a mass $\mathrm{m}$ as shown. The frequency of oscillation of the mass is $\mathrm{f}$. If both $k_1$ and $k_2$ are made four times their original values, the frequency of oscillation becomes
question: Two springs, of force constants $k_1$ and $k_2$, are connected to a mass $\mathrm{m}$ as shown. The frequency of oscillation of the mass is $\mathrm{f}$. If both $k_1$ and $k_2$ are made four times their original values, the frequency of oscillation becomes
solution: $\begin{aligned} f & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{k_1+k_2}{m}} \\ f^{\prime} & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} 2 \sqrt{\frac{k_1+k_2}{m}}=2 f\end{aligned}$
